serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mama Mia Mutton (Spicy) is approximately 200 g, served on a sizzling tawa (hot iron plate) as a main dish at an Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mama Mia Mutton (Spicy) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mama Mia Mutton (Spicy), cr_id G00010899, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (USDA NDB 17290) as a secondary reference; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mama Mia Mutton (Spicy) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-and-vegetable curry dishes with moderate carbohydrate content (7.44g/100g), consistent with published GI values for meat-based Indian curries in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 200g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2.0) / 100 = 5.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g with low potassium (210 mg/100g) and negligible dietary fibre (0.3 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–120 g per meal, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes, as a 200 g serving delivers approximately 934 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the 2400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (7.44 g/100g) and low sugar (4.69 g/100g) offset by high protein (14.05 g/100g) which slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (150–200 g) due to its low GI (~35) and high protein content; however, total meal carbohydrate load must be managed by avoiding co-consumption of large portions of white rice or roti.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.8 g/100g estimated) and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) from red meat, combined with high sodium (467 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100 g or less) due to the combined burden of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and high sodium; substituting with leaner proteins such as chicken or fish is advisable for regular meals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (mutton) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; sodium exceeds 400 mg/100g RA threshold; tomatoes are nightshades
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish as mutton is a primary pro-inflammatory red meat trigger; consider substituting with chicken or oily fish, and monitor individual joint response to nightshade ingredients (tomatoes).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mama Mia Mutton (Spicy) is a Singaporean Indian restaurant dish consisting of succulent pieces of mutton (lamb) cooked with chopped onions and tomatoes in a spiced masala gravy, served sizzling on a hot iron tawa (flat griddle plate). The dish is characterised by its bold, fiery flavour profile derived from chilli, cumin, coriander, and other Indian spices, with a tender, slightly charred texture from the tawa cooking method. It is a popular lunch and dinner option at Indian restaurants in Singapore, particularly within the North Indian and fusion Indian dining segment. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 467 mg. The high protein content supports satiety and glycaemic control, making it conditionally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. However, the elevated sodium level raises concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The red meat base (mutton) contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid, which is relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular risk. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore Indian restaurant contexts and is gluten-free by default. It pairs commonly with naan, roti, or steamed rice.
common_user_queries: Is Mama Mia Mutton spicy good for diabetes?
How much sodium is in tawa mutton?
Calories in spicy mutton Indian dish Singapore
Is mutton bad for high blood pressure?
Can I eat mutton curry with rheumatoid arthritis?
Spicy lamb dish Singapore Indian restaurant

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010899) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb data as a secondary reference. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology for meat-based Indian curries.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (NDB 17290) as the HPB record does not report saturated fat separately; this value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific tawa mutton preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this branded dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status is assumed based on typical Singapore Indian restaurant practice; formal certification should be verified with the establishment. 5. The dish description in the input contained concatenated text ('Succulentpiecesoflambcookedwithchoppedonionsandtomatoes') which has been normalised to standard English in this record.
